notes.text: Add a second STAT: marker or stat-box shortcode. You have strong quantitative claims throughout — the $630B Morgan Stanley figure, the 90% operating cash flow stat from Bank of America, and the 6-7% silicon wafer growth rate — any one of these would add visual diversity and reinforce credibility at a glance without changing word count materially.

Confirm your internal link slugs match published Particle Post URLs before this goes live. The anchor text 'enterprise AI ROI analysis' and 'Big Tech $700B AI data center analysis' are well-written, but if those slugs don't resolve, readers hit dead links and SEO equity is lost. Pull the actual slug values from your CMS and hard-code them into the href.

Fix the Forrester/Gartner misattribution before this goes live: the '50% of AI job cuts reversed by 2027' forecast is a Gartner prediction, not a Forrester finding. Forrester's verified stat is the 55% employer-regret figure. Mixing the two in a single sentence damages credibility with the CFO/COO audience who will recognize the error. Correct the attribution or separate the two claims clearly.

Trim approximately 150-200 words to land within the 1,800-3,000 MOF word count ceiling. The 'What This Deployment Does NOT Prove' section contains five parallel 'It does not prove...' constructions that are repetitive at this length — condensing those to three tightly worded bullets would preserve analytical rigor while cutting the overage and tightening the reader experience.

Replace the picsum.photos placeholder cover image with a licensed editorial image before scheduling publication. A placeholder image on a case study targeting CFOs and COOs signals draft status and undermines the authority positioning the article otherwise earns through its sourcing and depth.

Fix the Windsurf claim immediately — this is a publication-embarrassing factual error. The OpenAI-Windsurf acquisition talks were reported by CNBC in April 2025, not 2026, and the deal collapsed entirely (Cognition bought Windsurf in July 2025). Either update this to accurately describe what happened historically and why it's relevant context, or remove the reference. Publishing it as a present-tense 2026 event will damage credibility with any executive who follows AI M&A.

TOF articles must stay under 1,000 words — the FAQ section is the culprit here. Since has_faq=true requires the FAQ to render in the body, you need to trim the core article to roughly 650-700 words to leave room for the FAQ pairs. Audit every paragraph for redundancy: the three 'concrete steps' section largely repeats points already made in the answer-first paragraph above it. Consolidate.

Replace the picsum.photos placeholder cover image before resubmission. A published article at Particle Post must have a real, licensed image. Also convert the KEY TAKEAWAY from inline text to a proper blockquote or callout shortcode — visual formatting elements like this must render distinctly from body copy to serve their purpose for skimmable executive readers.
QA gate failed — fix programmatic issues before retry

RECONCILE THE AI USE-CASE COUNT: You cite '450 AI use cases in production' (attributed to Emerj) in one section and 'more than 500 active AI use cases' (attributed to Chronicle Journal, March 2026) in the closing section. Pick the most current verified figure (500+, per Chronicle Journal March 2026) and use it consistently throughout. Contradictory statistics in the same article undermine credibility with the executive audience this piece targets.

FIX THE STAT MARKER UNIT ERROR: 'STAT: 12,000 | Commercial credit agreements COiN processes per second' is factually wrong — COiN processes 12,000 annual agreements in seconds (total elapsed time), not 12,000 per second. Change the descriptor to 'Commercial credit agreements COiN processes annually, in seconds' to match what Bloomberg and ABA Journal actually reported. A single wrong unit in a visible callout is the kind of error that triggers reader distrust.

UPGRADE THE KEY TAKEAWAY TO A SHORTCODE: The KEY TAKEAWAY is buried as bold inline text mid-article. For a MOF case study targeting CFOs and COOs, this is the single most important scannable element on the page. Wrap it in the publication's callout shortcode (e.g., {{< callout >}}) so it renders as a visually distinct box. Executives who skim will miss it entirely in its current form, which defeats the purpose of including it.
QA gate failed — fix programmatic issues before retry

Tighten primary source attribution on the $55M figure: the article cites 'Investing.com citing company data' but Walmart's own July 2025 corporate newsroom post (already listed as Source #4) is the primary record. Replace the Investing.com attribution inline with 'according to Walmart's July 2025 global supply chain announcement' — this removes one degree of indirection and strengthens credibility with executive readers who will click through.

Clarify the Pactum metrics timeline to pre-empt reader skepticism: the original HBR-documented Walmart pilot (2022) reported a 64% agreement rate and 1.5% average savings. The article uses the higher program-level figures from Pactum's client page (68% / 3%). Add a single parenthetical — e.g., '(across the full program; the initial 2022 pilot reported 64% and 1.5%, per Harvard Business Review)' — so sophisticated COO readers don't flag the discrepancy as an error.

Add at least one blockquote callout (a pull-quote from CFO Rainey or CEO McMillon) to satisfy MOF visual diversity standards more robustly. The CFO's Q3 FY2025 earnings language — 'That translates into lower shipping costs' — or the 'obvious benefits for per-unit delivery cost' line already in the article body are ready-made candidates. This closes the minor visual element gap and increases on-page engagement for the executive audience.
QA gate failed — fix programmatic issues before retry

Trim to BOF word count (1,200–2,000 words): At roughly 2,800 words, this article is significantly over the BOF ceiling. The 'Success Metrics' and 'What Does AI Workforce Planning Actually Cost?' sections are strong but could be condensed into a single table or bullet list rather than full prose paragraphs. The FAQ section, while excellent, adds ~400 words — consider moving it to a linked companion piece or cutting to 3 Q&A pairs maximum. Getting under 2,000 words will sharpen the BOF conversion focus.

Fix the has_faq frontmatter flag: The YAML says 'has_faq: false' but the article contains a full, well-written FAQ section. Change this to 'has_faq: true' before publication. This controls whether the HowTo/FAQ schema markup renders in search results — with it set to false, you are leaving rich result eligibility on the table for every FAQ entry, which is a significant SEO loss for a BOF piece targeting high-intent search queries.

Strengthen the SHRM 20-35% attrition reduction citation with a direct quote or page reference: The claim 'AI workforce planning cuts voluntary attrition by 20 to 35%, according to SHRM's 2026 State of AI in HR Report' is the article's anchor stat in both the lede and the second STAT: marker, but publicly available SHRM excerpts do not surface this exact range. Add a page number, quote the finding verbatim, or supplement with a second corroborating source (e.g., Visier or McKinsey) so the claim is bulletproof against editorial challenge or reader scrutiny.

TRIM TO TOF RANGE: The article body runs approximately 50-100 words over the 1,000-word TOF ceiling. Cut the 'Three Steps That Close Most of the Exposure' section by condensing each bullet to one tight sentence, or fold it into the preceding H2. This section reads like the start of a MOF piece and is the primary reason the count is over.

ADD AN EXPLICIT VERDICT SECTION: TOF compliance requires a labeled 'Clear Verdict' or '## Verdict' H2. Right now the closing paragraphs do the work but won't satisfy checklist item 8 or the TOF structural requirement. Add a 2-3 sentence verdict H2 before the internal links — something like '## Is AI Washing a Legal Risk Worth Prioritizing Now?' — and answer it in a single direct paragraph.

CITE OR CUT THE WSJ REFERENCE: The inline citation 'according to reporting by The Wall Street Journal' is the only claim in the article without a corresponding URL in the Sources section. Either add the specific WSJ article URL to the Sources list or rephrase the claim to cite a documentable primary source (e.g., SEC enforcement docket). Unsourced WSJ references create liability for the publication if the characterization is disputed.

Fix the duplicate paragraph problem before resubmission: the formatter appears to be stacking two versions of content in at least two sections ('Machine Learning Credit Scoring' and 'Agentic AI Compliance'). Each factual claim should appear exactly once. This is the single most damaging structural flaw and is an automatic -20 under the scoring rubric.

This article is clearly written at MOF depth and length — it names multiple friction scenarios, includes regulatory citations, and builds a nuanced argument. Refile it as MOF (target 1,800-3,000 words), expand the commercial lending friction scenarios to two full named examples, and add a second internal link. The content quality is strong enough to earn a high MOF score with minimal additional writing.

Manually verify the Upstart 91% automated decisioning figure against the actual 2023 10-K filing before publication — the figure is directionally consistent with Upstart's public disclosures, but the exact percentage and framing should be confirmed against the primary document, not a secondary summary. Similarly, confirm OCC Bulletin 2023-22 is the correct bulletin number for model risk management guidance, as OCC bulletin numbering is frequently misremembered in AI-generated drafts.
QA gate failed — fix programmatic issues before retry

Cut aggressively to hit the 1,800–3,000 word MOF ceiling. The 'Production-Ready vs. Experimental' section and the 'What to Watch Over the Next 24 Months' section are strong candidates for trimming or removal — they add texture but dilute the core vendor-selection argument. Alternatively, consider splitting this into a two-part series: a comparative framework piece (MOF) and a forward-looking outlook piece (TOF). The word count violation is the only structural issue holding this article back from a clean score.

Add at least one blockquote callout to satisfy the MOF visual diversity requirement. The KEY TAKEAWAY paragraph is well-written and already formatted inline — converting it to a proper blockquote shortcode would close this gap instantly and improve scannability for executive readers who skim before committing to a full read.

The six-criteria scoring table is this article's most defensible and shareable asset, but the methodology behind the 1–5 scores is author-attributed rather than third-party sourced. Add a brief methodology footnote explaining that scores reflect editorial assessment of documented production deployments as of Q1 2026, or anchor at least one score per vendor to a named external source. This protects the publication if a vendor challenges the scores publicly.
